title: SEGMENT 17 — Neoantigen vaccines plus checkpoint inhibitors

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Neoantigen vaccines plus checkpoint inhibitors

I want to spend this segment on one specific combination strategy, because I think it represents one of the most exciting near-term frontiers in cancer therapy: the combination of personalized cancer vaccines with checkpoint inhibitors.
Remember the scaffold.
Checkpoint inhibitors work when T cells are already present in the tumor.

If you could make T cells present — generate, on demand, new T cells specifically targeting the patient's own tumor antigens — and then release the checkpoint brakes — you'd have a one-two punch that might extend the benefits of immunotherapy to many more patients, and might convert cold tumors into hot ones.
That is exactly what personalized neoantigen vaccines are designed to do.
The mechanism is beautiful.
You take a patient's tumor and sequence it.
You compare the tumor's DNA to the patient's normal DNA.
You identify the mutations that are unique to the tumor.

You then use prediction algorithms to figure out which of those mutated proteins are most likely to be presented on the patient's specific MHC molecules — because every patient has different MHC alleles, and different alleles bind different peptides.
You pick, say, twenty to thirty of the most promising predicted neoantigens.
You synthesize an mRNA molecule that, when injected into the patient, encodes those neoantigens.
The mRNA gets taken up by the patient's own dendritic cells, translated into protein, processed, displayed on MHC.
T cells against those neoantigens get primed.
The patient now has a T-cell army specifically targeting peptides that exist only in their own cancer.
Then, you give the patient pembrolizumab.
The newly primed T cells, equipped with neoantigen-specific receptors, traffic to the tumor.
They encounter the same mutated peptides being presented by tumor cells.
They engage their targets.
And the PD-1 blockade prevents the tumor from shutting them down.
The lead clinical candidate, as of 2025, is mRNA-4157, also called V940, from Moderna and Merck.
It's a personalized mRNA vaccine encoding up to thirty-four neoantigens per patient, manufactured individually based on each patient's tumor sequencing.
In a Phase 2 trial in melanoma, published in 2023, patients with high-risk resected melanoma were randomized to mRNA-4157 plus pembrolizumab versus pembrolizumab alone.
The combination reduced the risk of recurrence or death by about forty-four percent compared to pembrolizumab alone.
In a cancer where pembrolizumab monotherapy had already set the bar high, the vaccine combination raised it further.
Phase 3 trials in melanoma and lung cancer are ongoing as of 2025.

If they confirm the Phase 2 results, mRNA-4157 plus pembrolizumab will become a new standard of care in adjuvant melanoma, and almost certainly the template for similar combinations in many other cancers.
There are competitors.
BNT122, from BioNTech and Genentech, is a similar personalized mRNA vaccine being tested in pancreatic cancer, colorectal cancer, and other settings.

Early data in pancreatic cancer — historically one of the hardest cancers to crack with immunotherapy — have been provocative, with vaccine-induced T-cell responses correlating with longer recurrence-free survival.
A randomized Phase 2 trial is reading out in the next year or two.
The mRNA-vaccine plus checkpoint combination is, in my view, the most exciting near-term development in immuno-oncology.
It rests on technology — mRNA manufacturing, neoantigen prediction algorithms, individualized therapy delivery — that did not exist a decade ago.
It potentially extends immunotherapy benefit to settings where current drugs do not work.
And it is being driven not by speculation but by Phase 2 randomized data showing real benefit.

If the Phase 3 results hold up, we are looking at a future where every patient with high-risk solid cancer gets, as part of standard care, a personalized vaccine manufactured from their own tumor's mutations, paired with a checkpoint inhibitor.
The vocabulary of cancer therapy will shift from "what kind of cancer do you have" to "what are your tumor's specific mutations and what vaccine do we need to make for you.
That is, in some sense, the realization of the personalized-medicine promise that has been advertised for thirty years.
It took mRNA technology, neoantigen prediction, and checkpoint inhibitors to come together.
They are coming together now.
